answer:
**The matlaʿ (opening couplet) of *Bang-e-Dara* Part 1, chapter 13, states:**

> Zahir ki aankh se na tamasha kare koi  
> Ho dekhna to deeda-e-dil wa kare koi[^1]

Its claim is a caution against superficial, outward observation (*zahīr kī āñkh se tamāshā*) of spectacles—likely worldly phenomena, divine realities, or mystical experiences—and an exhortation to perceive truly only through the "eye of the heart" (*dīdā-e-dil*), emphasizing inner, spiritual vision over literal sight.[^2]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 13 ("Zahir ki aankh se na tamasha kare koi") [paragraph](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/13-zahir-ki-aankh-se-na-tamasha-kare-koi/transliteration/#p-464c98).

[^2]: The poem continues this theme, referencing the mystic Mansoor (al-Hallaj), who faced execution for ecstatic declarations of divine union, underscoring that true love (*ishq*) demands inner sight, not claims provable by outward eyes.[^1] No English translation is available in the corpus for this chapter (only original and transliteration variants).
